preamble: The shift from the velar nasal [ŋ] to the alveolar nasal [n], often colloquially called 'G-dropping', is a common sociolinguistic phenomenon in English and other languages. It typically occurs in unstressed syllables where the place of articulation moves forward from the soft palate to the alveolar ridge.

examples.note: Widespread in casual registers, Southern American English, AAVE, and various British accents.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English. Cambridge University Press.
Labov, W. (2001). Principles of Linguistic Change.
